questions.prompt: Why use OIDC instead of long-lived AWS keys for deploys?

choices.text: OIDC (OpenID Connect) gives the workflow a short-lived token authenticated as a specific AWS role — no long-lived credentials stored in GitHub Secrets. The cloud provider trusts GitHub as an identity provider; each workflow run gets a fresh token scoped to one role. If GitHub is compromised, attackers can't grab a permanent key. If the workflow needs new permissions, you update the IAM role (not rotate keys everywhere). Setup once via aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials with `role-to-assume`; works for all future workflows. Standard pattern in 2026 across AWS, GCP, Azure. Hugely improves security posture vs the old approach of stuffing AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID in repository secrets.

questions.explanation: Short-lived tokens via OIDC; no long-lived creds in GitHub. Standard 2026 pattern.

questions.prompt: Want auto-deploy to staging on every main push but human approval required for production. How?

choices.text: Two workflows. Staging workflow triggers on `push` to main, deploys automatically using staging-scoped secrets (in a `staging` environment). Production workflow triggers on `push` of version tags (`tags: ['v*']`) or `workflow_dispatch` (manual button), deploys using a `production` environment — and that environment in repo settings has 'Required reviewers' configured. The workflow pauses at the production environment gate; the reviewer gets notified; clicking 'Approve' lets it proceed. Combine with `concurrency` to prevent overlapping prod deploys. Human in the loop for production without slowing down staging iteration.

questions.explanation: Separate workflows; production environment with required reviewers pauses deploy until approved.
